solution_text: (a, b) Key concept: We define the magnetic moment of the current loop as, m or {tex}\mu_{i}{/tex} = IA ...(i) The electron performs uniform circular motion around a stationary heavy nucleus of charge + Ze. This constitutes a current I. {tex}I=\frac{e}{T}{/tex} and T is the time period of revolution. Let r be the orbital radius of the electron, and v the orbital speed. Then, {tex}T=\frac{2 \pi r}{v}{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{e v}{2 \pi r}{/tex} and {tex}A=\pi r^{2}{/tex} So; by substituting this in equation (i), we get {tex}\mu_{l}=\frac{e v r}{2}{/tex} and by multiplying-dividing this equation with m e we get {tex}\mu_{l}=\frac{-e}{2 m_{e}} L{/tex} gyromagnetic ratio is given by =magnetic moment/angular momentum =-e/2m e . ,which is a constant thus it satisy (a) and (b) condition.

(a, b)
Key concept: We define the magnetic moment of the current loop as,
m or {tex}\mu_{i}{/tex} = IA ...(i)

{tex}I=\frac{e}{T}{/tex}
and T is the time period of revolution. Let r be the orbital radius of the electron, and v the orbital speed. Then,
{tex}T=\frac{2 \pi r}{v}{/tex}
{tex}I=\frac{e v}{2 \pi r}{/tex} and {tex}A=\pi r^{2}{/tex}

question_text: Consider a wire carrying a steady current, I placed in a uniform magnetic field B perpendicular to its length. Consider the charges inside the wire. It is known that magnetic forces do no work. This implies that, the motion of charges inside the conductor is unaffected by B since they do not absorb energy. some charges inside the wire move to the surface as a result of B. if the wire moves under the influence of B, no work is done by the force. if the wire moves under the influence of B, no work is done by the magnetic force on the ions, assumed fixed within the wire.
question_html:
Consider a wire carrying a steady current, I placed in a uniform magnetic field B perpendicular to its length. Consider the charges inside the wire. It is known that magnetic forces do no work. This implies that,

  1. the motion of charges inside the conductor is unaffected by B since they do not absorb energy.
  2. some charges inside the wire move to the surface as a result of B.
  3. if the wire moves under the influence of B, no work is done by the force.
  4. if the wire moves under the influence of B, no work is done by the magnetic force on the ions, assumed fixed within the wire.
solution_text: (b, d). Key concept: If a current-carrying straight conductor (the length I) is placed in a uniform magnetic field (B) such that it makes an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the direction of field then force experienced by it is F max = {tex}\vec B \vec i \vec l{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} . Direction of this force is obtained by right hand palm rule. Right-hand palm rule: Stretch the fingers and thumb of the right hand at right angles to each other. Then if the fingers point in the direction of field B and thumb in the direction of current z, then normal to the palm will point in the direction of the force. If the conductor is placed perpendicular to the magnetic field, then {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90°, F max = {tex}\vec B \vec I \vec l{/tex} Motion of charges inside the conductor is affected by magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} , due to magnetic force {tex}\vec F{/tex} , given by =q x( {tex}\vec v{/tex} x {tex}\vec B{/tex} ).discard option (a).
solution_html:
(b, d). Key concept: If a current-carrying straight conductor (the length I) is placed in a uniform magnetic field (B) such that it makes an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the direction of field then force experienced by it is Fmax = {tex}\vec B \vec i \vec l{/tex} sin{tex}\theta{/tex}. Direction of this force is obtained by right hand palm rule.
Right-hand palm rule: Stretch the fingers and thumb of the right hand at right angles to each other. Then if the fingers point in the direction of field B and thumb in the direction of current z, then normal to the palm will point in the direction of the force.

If the conductor is placed perpendicular to the magnetic field, then {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90°, Fmax = {tex}\vec B \vec I \vec l{/tex}
Motion of charges inside the conductor is affected by magnetic field{tex}\vec B{/tex} , due to magnetic force {tex}\vec F{/tex}, given by =q x({tex}\vec v{/tex}x{tex}\vec B{/tex}).discard option (a).

question_text: Two identical current-carrying coaxial loops, carry current I in an opposite sense. A simple amperian loop passes through both of them once. Calling the loop as C, {tex}\oint_c {\mathbf{\vec B}} \cdot {\mathbf{d \vec l}} = \pm 2{\mu _0}I{/tex} the value of {tex}\oint_c {\mathbf{\vec B}} \cdot {\mathbf{d \vec l}}{/tex} is independent of sense of C. there may be a point on C where B and dl are perpendiculars B vanishes everywhere on C.
question_html:
Two identical current-carrying coaxial loops, carry current I in an opposite sense. A simple amperian loop passes through both of them once. Calling the loop as C,

  1. {tex}\oint_c {\mathbf{\vec B}} \cdot {\mathbf{d \vec l}} = \pm 2{\mu _0}I{/tex}
  2. the value of {tex}\oint_c {\mathbf{\vec B}} \cdot {\mathbf{d \vec l}}{/tex} is independent of sense of C.
  3. there may be a point on C where B and dl are perpendiculars
  4. B vanishes everywhere on C.
solution_text: (b, c) Key concept: Ampere's law gives another method to calculate the magnetic field due to a given current distribution. The line integral of the magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} around any closed curve is equal to {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} times the net current i threading through the area enclosed by the curve, i.e., {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d I}=\mu_{0}{/tex} {tex}\Sigma i=\mu_{0}\left(i_{1}+i_{3}-i_{2}\right){/tex} Total current crossing the above area is (i 1 + i 3 + i 2 ). Any current outside the area is not included in the net current. (Outward {tex}\odot \rightarrow+v e{/tex} , Inward {tex}\otimes \rightarrow-\text { ve }{/tex} ) Applying the Ampere's circuital law, we have {tex}\oint \mathbf{\vec B} \cdot \mathrm{d} \mathbf{\vec l}{/tex} {tex}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}_{0}{/tex} (I - I) = 0 (because the current is in the opposite sense.) Also, there may be a point on C where B and dl are perpendicular and hence, {tex}\oint_{c} \mathbf{\vec B} \cdot \mathbf{d \vec l}=0{/tex} The value of B does not vanish on various points of C. Thus option (d) is wrong
solution_html:
(b, c)
Key concept: Ampere's law gives another method to calculate the magnetic field due to a given current distribution.

The line integral of the magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} around any closed curve is equal to {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} times the net current i threading through the area enclosed by the curve, i.e., 
{tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d I}=\mu_{0}{/tex} {tex}\Sigma i=\mu_{0}\left(i_{1}+i_{3}-i_{2}\right){/tex}
Total current crossing the above area is (i1 + i3 + i2). Any current outside the area is not included in the net current. (Outward {tex}\odot \rightarrow+v e{/tex}, Inward {tex}\otimes \rightarrow-\text { ve }{/tex})
Applying the Ampere's circuital law, we have
{tex}\oint \mathbf{\vec B} \cdot \mathrm{d} \mathbf{\vec l}{/tex} {tex}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}_{0}{/tex} (I - I) = 0 (because the current is in the opposite sense.)
Also, there may be a point on C where B and dl are perpendicular and hence,
{tex}\oint_{c} \mathbf{\vec B} \cdot \mathbf{d \vec l}=0{/tex}

The value of B does not vanish on various points of C. Thus option (d) is wrong

question_text: A cubical region of space is filled with some uniform electric and magnetic fields. An electron enters the cube across one of its faces with velocity v and a positron enters via opposite face with velocity - v. At this instant. the electric forces on both the particles cause identical accelerations. the magnetic forces on both the particles cause equal accelerations. both particles gain or loose energy at the same rate. the motion of the centre of mass (CM) is determined by B alone.
question_html:
A cubical region of space is filled with some uniform electric and magnetic fields. An electron enters the cube across one of its faces with velocity v and a positron enters via opposite face with velocity - v. At this instant.

  1. the electric forces on both the particles cause identical accelerations.
  2. the magnetic forces on both the particles cause equal accelerations.
  3. both particles gain or loose energy at the same rate.
  4. the motion of the centre of mass (CM) is determined by B alone.
solution_text: (b), (c) and (d) are correct. Key concept: This problem is based upon the single moving charge placed with some uniform electric and magnetic fields in space. Then they experience a force called Lorentz force is given by the relation F net = qe + (v {tex}\times{/tex} B). The magnetic forces (F m = q (v {tex}\times{/tex} B), on charge particle, is either zero or Fm is perpendicular to v (or component of v) which in turn revolves particles on a circular path with uniform speed. In both cases, particles have equal accelerations. Due to the same electric force (F e = qE) which is in opposite direction (because of the sign of charge) both the particles gain or loss energy at the same rate. There is no change of the Centre of Mass (CM) of the particles, therefore the motion of the Centre of Mass (CM) is determined by B alone.
solution_html:
(b), (c) and (d) are correct.
Key concept: This problem is based upon the single moving charge placed with some uniform electric and magnetic fields in space. Then they experience a force called Lorentz force is given by the relation Fnet = qe + (v {tex}\times{/tex} B).

  2. Due to the same electric force (Fe = qE) which is in opposite direction (because of the sign of charge) both the particles gain or loss energy at the same rate.
  3. There is no change of the Centre of Mass (CM) of the particles, therefore the motion of the Centre of Mass (CM) is determined by B alone.

question_text: Verify that the cyclotron frequency {tex}\omega{/tex} = eB/m has the correct dimensions of [T] -1 .
question_html: Verify that the cyclotron frequency {tex}\omega{/tex} = eB/m has the correct dimensions of [T]-1.
solution_text: In cyclotron, charge particle describes the circular path where magnetic force acts as centripetal force. {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{R}{/tex} = evB On simplifying the terms, we have {tex}\therefore \frac{e B}{m}=\frac{v}{R}=\omega{/tex} We know that B {tex}=\frac{F}{e v}=\frac{\left[M L T^{-2}\right]}{[A T]\left[L T^{-1}\right]}{/tex} = MA -1 T -2 ] And, then dimensional formula of angular frequency {tex}\therefore [\omega]=\left[\frac{e B}{m}\right]=\left[\frac{v}{R}\right]{/tex} {tex}[\omega]=\frac{[A T]\left[M A^{-1} T^{-2}\right]}{[M]}{/tex} = [T -1 ] Hence both of the results are proved.
solution_html:
In cyclotron, charge particle describes the circular path where magnetic force acts as centripetal force.
{tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{R}{/tex} = evB
On simplifying the terms, we have
{tex}\therefore \frac{e B}{m}=\frac{v}{R}=\omega{/tex}
We know that B {tex}=\frac{F}{e v}=\frac{\left[M L T^{-2}\right]}{[A T]\left[L T^{-1}\right]}{/tex} = MA-1 T-2]
And,
then dimensional formula of angular frequency
{tex}\therefore [\omega]=\left[\frac{e B}{m}\right]=\left[\frac{v}{R}\right]{/tex}
{tex}[\omega]=\frac{[A T]\left[M A^{-1} T^{-2}\right]}{[M]}{/tex} = [T-1] Hence both of the results are proved.

question_text: Show that a force that does no work must be a velocity dependent force.
question_html: Show that a force that does no work must be a velocity dependent force.
solution_text: The work done by a force is given by dW = F.dl = Fdl cos {tex}\theta{/tex} As work done by force is zero, so dW = F.dl = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}F \cdot \frac{d l}{d t} \times d t=0{/tex} dW = F.v dt = 0 as dt {tex}\ne{/tex} 0 [ {tex}\therefore{/tex} F.v = 0] So F must be velocity dependent, i.e., angle between F and v must be 90 o always, then for F.v = 0 Fv cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = cos 90 o {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90 o If v changes direction then to make {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90 o , F must change angle according to v. So F is dependent of v to make work done zero.
solution_html:
The work done by a force is given by dW = F.dl = Fdl cos {tex}\theta{/tex}
As work done by force is zero, so
dW = F.dl = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}F \cdot \frac{d l}{d t} \times d t=0{/tex}
dW = F.v dt = 0
as dt {tex}\ne{/tex} 0 [{tex}\therefore{/tex} F.v = 0]
So F must be velocity dependent, i.e., angle between F and v must be 90o always, then for F.v = 0
Fv cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = cos 90o
{tex}\theta{/tex} = 90o
If v changes direction then to make {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90o, F must change angle according to v. So F is dependent of v to make work done zero.

question_text: Describe the motion of a charged particle in a cyclotron if the frequency of the radio frequency (rf) field were doubled.
question_html: Describe the motion of a charged particle in a cyclotron if the frequency of the radio frequency (rf) field were doubled.
solution_text: The frequency v a of the applied voltage (radio frequency) is adjusted so that the polarity of the dees is reversed in the same time that it takes the ions to complete one half of the revolution. The requirement v a = v c is called the resonance condition. When the frequency of the radio frequency (rf) field was doubled, then the resonance condition is violated and the time period of the radio frequency (rf) field was halved. Therefore, the duration in which the particle completes half revolution inside the dees, radiofrequency completes the cycle. So, the particle will accelerate and decelerate alternatively, hence the particle moves in a circular path. So, the radius of the path in the dees will remain the same.

When the frequency of the radio frequency (rf) field was doubled, then the resonance condition is violated and the time period of the radio frequency (rf) field was halved. Therefore, the duration in which the particle completes half revolution inside the dees, radiofrequency completes the cycle. So, the particle will accelerate and decelerate alternatively, hence the particle moves in a circular path. So, the radius of the path in the dees will remain the same.

question_text: Two long wires carrying current I 1 and I 2 are arranged as shown in Fig. The one carrying current I 1 is along the x-axis. The other carrying current I 2 is along a line parallel to the y-axis given by x = 0 and z = d. Find the force exerted at O 2 because of the wire along the x-axis.
question_html: Two long wires carrying current I1 and I2 are arranged as shown in Fig. The one carrying current I1 is along the x-axis. The other carrying current I2 is along a line parallel to the y-axis given by x = 0 and z = d. Find the force exerted at O2 because of the wire along the x-axis.
solution_text: We know that force on current (I) carrying conductor placed in magnetic field B is F = I(L {tex}\times{/tex} B) = ILB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} The direction of the magnetic field at O 2 due to the current I 1 is parallel to Y-axis and in -Y direction. As wire of current I 2 is parallel to Y-axis, current in I 2 is also along Y-axis. So I 2 and B 1 (magnetic field due to current I 1 ) are along Y-axis i.e., the angle between I 2 and B 1 is zero. So, magnetic force F 2 on wire of current I 2 is F 2 = B 2 I 2 L 1 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ° = 0 , thus resultant force = 0
solution_html:
We know that force on current (I) carrying conductor placed in magnetic field B is
F = I(L {tex}\times{/tex} B) = ILB sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
The direction of the magnetic field at O2 due to the current I1 is parallel to Y-axis and in -Y direction.
As wire of current I2 is parallel to Y-axis, current in I2 is also along Y-axis. So I2 and B1 (magnetic field due to current I1) are along Y-axis i.e., the angle between I2 and B1 is zero.
So, magnetic force F2 on wire of current I2 is F2 = B2 I2 L1 sin {tex}\theta{/tex}° = 0 , thus resultant force = 0

question_text: A current-carrying loop consists of 3 identical quarter circles of radius R, lying in the positive quadrants of the X-Y, Y-Z and Z-X planes with their centers at the origin, joined together. Find the direction and magnitude of B at the origin.
question_html: A current-carrying loop consists of 3 identical quarter circles of radius R, lying in the positive quadrants of the X-Y, Y-Z and Z-X planes with their centers at the origin, joined together. Find the direction and magnitude of B at the origin.
solution_text: Consider in the figure, 3 quadrants of conductors AB, BC, and CD along positive X-Y, Y-Z and Z-X planes respectively. A and D are connected to a battery which is responsible to flow current I through the three quadrants of radius R coordinate of A or D (R,0,0), B(0, R,0) and of C(0,0,R). Now the direction of the magnetic field by right-hand thumb rule due to quadrants AB, BC and CD are +B 1 , B 2 and B 3 along +Z, +X and +Y directions respectively. So, in the centre of the quadrant. {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}_{\vec{B}}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi R} \cdot \frac{\frac{\pi}{2}}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{8 \pi R}{/tex} So Magnetic field due to quadrants AB, BC, and CD at their centre O are B 1 , B 2, and B 3 respectively is given below:- {tex}B_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{8 \pi R} \hat{k} \quad B_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{8 \pi R} \hat{i}{/tex} and {tex}B_{3}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{8 \pi R} \hat{j}{/tex} So-net magnetic field at origin due to three current-carrying loops B = B 1 + B 2 + B 3 {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{8 R}[\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}]{/tex} , resultant magnetic field The resultant of B 1 , B 2 and B 3 will be diagonal OR of the cube of side B 1 , B 2 , B 3 as the |B 1 |=|B 2 |=|B 3 |
solution_html:
Consider in the figure, 3 quadrants of conductors AB, BC, and CD along positive X-Y, Y-Z and Z-X planes respectively. A and D are connected to a battery which is responsible to flow current I through the three quadrants of radius R coordinate of A or D (R,0,0), B(0, R,0) and of C(0,0,R). Now the direction of the magnetic field by right-hand thumb rule due to quadrants AB, BC and CD are +B1, B2 and B3 along +Z, +X and +Y directions respectively. So, in the centre of the quadrant.

{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}_{\vec{B}}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi R} \cdot \frac{\frac{\pi}{2}}{2 \pi}{/tex}
{tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{8 \pi R}{/tex}
So Magnetic field due to quadrants AB, BC, and CD at their centre O are B1, B2, and B3 respectively is given below:-
{tex}B_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{8 \pi R} \hat{k} \quad B_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{8 \pi R} \hat{i}{/tex} and {tex}B_{3}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{8 \pi R} \hat{j}{/tex}
So-net magnetic field at origin due to three current-carrying loops B = B1 + B2 + B3
{tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{8 R}[\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}]{/tex} , resultant magnetic field
The resultant of B1, B2 and B3 will be diagonal OR of the cube of side B1, B2, B3 as the |B1|=|B2|=|B3|

question_html: A charged particle of charge e and mass m is moving in an electric field E and magnetic field B. Construct dimensionless quantities and quantities of dimension [T]-1.
solution_text: When a charged particle is placed in an electric and magnetic field, its starts moving in helical motion where electric field E and B is perpendicular to direction of motion, electric field exert force of charged particle and centripetal force is applied by magnetic force. F m = qvB sin 90° = qvB ...(i) and centripetal force is= {tex}mv^2\over R{/tex} ....(ii) By eq, (i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{R}=q v B{/tex} {tex}\frac{v}{R}=\frac{q B}{m}{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} v = {tex}\omega{/tex} R and q = e angular velocity. {tex}\omega=\frac{v}{R}=\frac{eB}{m}{/tex} Dimensional formula for angular velocity {tex}\omega{/tex} {tex}\omega=\left[\frac{e B}{m}\right]=\left[T^{-1}\right]{/tex}

When a charged particle is placed in an electric and magnetic field, its starts moving in helical motion where electric field E and B is perpendicular to direction of motion, electric field exert force of charged particle and centripetal force is applied by magnetic force.

{tex}\frac{v}{R}=\frac{q B}{m}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} v = {tex}\omega{/tex}R and q = e
angular velocity.

question_text: An electron enters with a velocity v = v 0 {tex}\mathop i\limits^ \wedge {/tex} into a cubical region (faces parallel to coordinate planes) in which there are uniform electric and magnetic fields. The orbit of the electron is found to spiral down inside the cube in plane parallel to the x-y plane. Suggest a configuration of fields E and B that can lead to it.
question_html: An electron enters with a velocity v = v0{tex}\mathop i\limits^ \wedge {/tex} into a cubical region (faces parallel to coordinate planes) in which there are uniform electric and magnetic fields. The orbit of the electron is found to spiral down inside the cube in plane parallel to the x-y plane. Suggest a configuration of fields E and B that can lead to it.
solution_text: Due to magnetic force charge particle revolves in a uniform circular motion in x-y plane and due to electric field charge particle increases the speed along x-direction, which in turn increases the radius of a circular path and hence, particle traversed on spiral path. Let us consider a magnetic field B = B 0 present in the region and an electron enters with a velocity into cubical region (faces parallel to coordinate planes). The force on electron, using magnetic Lorentz force, is given by {tex}\vec{F}=-e\left(v_{0} \hat{i} \times B_{0} \hat{k}\right){/tex} {tex}=e v_{0} B_{0} \hat{j}{/tex} which revolves the electron in x-y plane. and E 0 = v 0 B 0 The electric force F = eE 0 J accelerates e along z-axis which in turn increases the radius of the circular path and hence particle traversed on spiral orbit.
solution_html:
Due to magnetic force charge particle revolves in a uniform circular motion in x-y plane and due to electric field charge particle increases the speed along x-direction, which in turn increases the radius of a circular path and hence, particle traversed on spiral path.
Let us consider a magnetic field B = B0 present in the region and an electron enters with a velocity into cubical region (faces parallel to coordinate planes). The force on electron, using magnetic Lorentz force, is given by
{tex}\vec{F}=-e\left(v_{0} \hat{i} \times B_{0} \hat{k}\right){/tex} {tex}=e v_{0} B_{0} \hat{j}{/tex}
which revolves the electron in x-y plane. and  E0 = v0B0
The electric force F = eE0J accelerates e along z-axis which in turn increases the radius of the circular path and hence particle traversed on spiral orbit.

question_text: A multirange voltmeter can be constructed by using a galvanometer circuit as shown in Fig. We want to construct a voltmeter that can measure 2V, 20V and 200V using a galvanometer of resistance 10Ω and that produces maximum deflection for current of 1 mA. Find R 1 , R 2 and R 3 that have to be used.
question_html: A multirange voltmeter can be constructed by using a galvanometer circuit as shown in Fig. We want to construct a voltmeter that can measure 2V, 20V and 200V using a galvanometer of resistance 10Ω and that produces maximum deflection for current of 1 mA. Find R1, R2 and R3 that have to be used.
solution_text: Key concept: The galvanometer can also be used as a voltmeter to measure the voltage across a given section of the circuit. For this a very high resistance wire is to be connected in series with galvanometer. The relationship is given by l G (G + R) =V where lg is the range of galvanometer, G is the resistance of galvanometer and R is the resistance of wire connected in series with galvanometer. Applying expression in different situations For i G (R 1 + G) = 2 for 2 V range For i G (G + R 1 + R 2 ) = 20 for 20 V range And For i G (G + R 1 + R 2 + R 3 ) = 200 for 200 v range By solving, we get R 1 = 1990 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , R 2 = 18 k {tex}\Omega{/tex} and R 3 = 180 K {tex}\Omega{/tex} . these are the required values.

question_text: A long straight wire carrying current of 25A rests on a table as shown in Fig. Another wire PQ of length 1m, mass 2.5 g carries the same current but in the opposite direction. The wire PQ is free to slide up and down. To what height will PQ rise?
question_html: A long straight wire carrying current of 25A rests on a table as shown in Fig. Another wire PQ of length 1m, mass 2.5 g carries the same current but in the opposite direction. The wire PQ is free to slide up and down. To what height will PQ rise?
solution_text: Key concept: The force applied on PQ by a long straight wire carrying current of 25 A which rests on a table. And the forces which other are repulsive if two straight wires are placed parallel to each other carrying current in opposite direction Now if the wire PQ is in equilibrium then that repulsive force on PQ must balance its weight.mass=25g,length=1 m The magnetic field produced by a long straight wire carrying current of 25 A rests on a table on small wire. {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi h}{/tex} The magnetic force on small conductor is F = Bl {tex}l{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = Bl {tex}l{/tex} and sin90 0 =1 Force applied on PQ balance the weight of small current carrying wire, F = mg {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} I^{2} l}{2 \pi h}{/tex} {tex}h=\frac{\mu_{0} I^{2} l}{2 \pi m g}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times(25)^{2} \times 1}{2 \pi \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.8}=51 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{m}{/tex} h = 0.51 cm.

And the forces which other are repulsive if two straight wires are placed parallel to each other carrying current in opposite direction Now if the wire PQ is in equilibrium then that repulsive force on PQ must balance its weight.mass=25g,length=1 m

The magnetic field produced by a long straight wire carrying current of 25 A rests on a table on small wire. 
{tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi h}{/tex}

F = Bl{tex}l{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = Bl{tex}l{/tex} and sin900=1
Force applied on PQ balance the weight of small current carrying wire,

{tex}h=\frac{\mu_{0} I^{2} l}{2 \pi m g}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times(25)^{2} \times 1}{2 \pi \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.8}=51 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
h = 0.51 cm.

A current 4.9 A passes through the coil and a constant magnetic field of 0.2 T acting inward (in xz plane) is switched on such that only arm CD of length 1 cm lies in the field. How much additional mass ‘m’ must be added to regain the balance?
question_html: A 100 turn rectangular coil ABCD (in XY plane) is hung from one arm of a balance (Fig.). A mass 500g is added to the other arm to balance the weight of the coil. solution_text: Key concept: Here we use the concept of magnetic force on straight current carrying conductor placed in the region of external uniform magnetic field. The magnetic force exerted on CD due to external magnetic field must balance its weight And spring balance to be in equilibrium net torque should also be equal to zero. At t = 0, the external magnetic field is off. Let us consider the separation of each hung from mid-point be l. Mg {tex}l{/tex} = W coil {tex}l{/tex} (by compairing the equations) 0.5 g= W coil W coil = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 9.8 N .........(i) By taking moment of force about mid-point, we get the weight of coil. And let 'm' be the mass which is added to regain the balance. When the magnetic field is switched on. Mg {tex}l{/tex} + mg {tex}l{/tex} = W coil {tex}l{/tex} + (ILB sin 90°) {tex}l{/tex} , from the first equation we get , mg {tex}l{/tex} = (ILB) {tex}l{/tex} {tex}m=\frac{B I L}{g}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{0.2 \times 4.9 \times 1 \times 10^{-2}}{9.8}{/tex} = 10 -3 kg = 1 g Therefore, 1 g of additional mass must be added to regain the balance.

question_text: A rectangular conducting loop consists of two wires on two opposite sides of length l joined together by rods of length d. The wires are each of the same material but with cross-sections differing by a factor of 2. The thicker wire has a resistance R and the rods are of low resistance, which in turn are connected to a constant voltage source V 0 . The loop is placed in uniform a magnetic field B at 45° to its plane. Find {tex}\tau{/tex} , the torque exerted by the magnetic field on the loop about an axis through the centres of rods.
question_html: A rectangular conducting loop consists of two wires on two opposite sides of length l joined together by rods of length d. The wires are each of the same material but with cross-sections differing by a factor of 2. The thicker wire has a resistance R and the rods are of low resistance, which in turn are connected to a constant voltage source V0. The loop is placed in uniform a magnetic field B at 45° to its plane. Find {tex}\tau{/tex}, the torque exerted by the magnetic field on the loop about an axis through the centres of rods.
solution_text: After analyzing the direction of current in both wires magnetic forces and torques need to be calculated for finding the net torque. A 1 =A and R 1 =2R and A 2 =2A and R 2 =R According to the problem, the thicker wire has a resistance R, then the other wire has a resistance 2R as the wires are of the same material so their resistivity remains same. Now, the force and hence, torque on first wire is given by F i = i 1 {tex}l{/tex} Bsin 90° {tex}=\frac{V_{0}}{2 R} l B{/tex} {tex}\tau_{1}=\frac{d}{2 \sqrt{2}} F_{1}=\frac{V_{0} l B d}{4 \sqrt{2} R}{/tex} Similarly, the force hence torque on other wire is given by F 2 = i 2 {tex}l{/tex} B sin 90° {tex}=\frac{V_{0}}{ R_{2}} l B{/tex} {tex}\tau_{2}=\frac{d}{2 \sqrt{2}} F_{2}=\frac{V_{0} l d B}{2\sqrt{2} R}{/tex} So, net torque, {tex}\tau=\tau_{2}-\tau_{1}{/tex} clockwise {tex}\tau=\frac{1}{4 \sqrt{2}} \frac{V_{0} A B}{R}{/tex} where A is the area of rectangular coil.

A1=A and R1=2R and A2=2A and R2=R
According to the problem, the thicker wire has a resistance R, then the other wire has a resistance 2R as the wires are of the same material so their resistivity remains same.
Now, the force and hence, torque on first wire is given by
Fi = i1 {tex}l{/tex}Bsin 90° {tex}=\frac{V_{0}}{2 R} l B{/tex}
{tex}\tau_{1}=\frac{d}{2 \sqrt{2}} F_{1}=\frac{V_{0} l B d}{4 \sqrt{2} R}{/tex}
Similarly, the force hence torque on other wire is given by

question_text: An electron and a positron are released from (0, 0, 0) and (0, 0, 1.5R ) respectively, in a uniform magnetic field B = B 0 {tex}\hat{\mathbf{i}}{/tex} , each with an equal momentum of magnitude p = e BR. Under what conditions on the direction of momentum will the orbits be non intersecting circles?
question_html: An electron and a positron are released from (0, 0, 0) and (0, 0, 1.5R ) respectively, in a uniform magnetic field B = B0 {tex}\hat{\mathbf{i}}{/tex}, each with an equal momentum of magnitude p = e BR. Under what conditions on the direction of momentum will the orbits be non intersecting circles?
solution_text: The magnetic field B is along the x-axis, hence for a circular orbit the momenta of the two particles are in the y-z plane. Let p 1 and p 2 be the momentum of the electron (e - ) and positron (e + ), respectively. Both traverse a circle of radius R of opposite sense. Let p, make an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with they-axis p 2 must make the same angle withy axis. The centres of the respective circles must be perpendicular to the momenta and at a distance R. Let the centre of the electron be at C e and of the positron at C p . The coordinates of C e is given by C e = (0, -R sin {tex}\theta{/tex} , R cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ) The coordinates of C p is given by C p = [0, -R sin {tex}\theta{/tex} , (1.5 R - R cos {tex}\theta{/tex} )] The circular orbits of electron and positron shall not overlap if the distance between the two centers are greater than 2R. Let d be the distance between C e and C p . Then, d 2 = [R sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - (-R sin {tex}\theta{/tex} )] 2 + [R cos {tex}\theta{/tex} - ( {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex} R - R cos {tex}\theta{/tex} )] 2 = (2 R sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ) 2 + (2 R cos {tex}\theta{/tex} - {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex} R) 2 = 4 R 2 sin2 {tex}\theta{/tex} + 4R 2 cos 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} - 6 R 2 cos 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} + {tex}\frac{9}{4}{/tex} R 2 = 4R 2 + {tex}\frac{9}{4}{/tex} R 2 - 6R 2 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} As d has to be greater than 2R, d 2 > 4R 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4R 2 + {tex}\frac{9}{4}{/tex} R 2 - 6R 2 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} > 4R 2 or, {tex}\frac{9}{4}{/tex} > 6 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} thus the conditions on the direction of momentum will the orbits be non-intersecting circles is cos {tex}\theta{/tex} < {tex}\frac{3}{8}{/tex}
solution_html:
The magnetic field B is along the x-axis, hence for a circular orbit the momenta of the two particles are in the y-z plane. Let p1 and p2 be the momentum of the electron (e-) and positron (e+), respectively. Both traverse a circle of radius R of opposite sense. Let p, make an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with they-axis p2 must make the same angle withy axis.
The centres of the respective circles must be perpendicular to the momenta and at a distance R. Let the centre of the electron be at Ce and of the positron at Cp.
The coordinates of Ce is given by  Ce = (0, -R sin {tex}\theta{/tex}, R cos {tex}\theta{/tex})
The coordinates of Cp is given by  Cp = [0, -R sin {tex}\theta{/tex}, (1.5 R - R cos {tex}\theta{/tex})]

The circular orbits of electron and positron shall not overlap if the distance between the two centers are greater than 2R.
Let d be the distance between Ce and Cp. Then,
d2 = [R sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - (-R sin {tex}\theta{/tex})]2 + [R cos {tex}\theta{/tex} - ({tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex} R - R cos {tex}\theta{/tex})]2
= (2 R sin {tex}\theta{/tex})2 + (2 R cos {tex}\theta{/tex} - {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex} R)2
= 4 R2 sin2 {tex}\theta{/tex} + 4R2 cos2 {tex}\theta{/tex} - 6 R2 cos2 {tex}\theta{/tex} + {tex}\frac{9}{4}{/tex} R2
= 4R2 + {tex}\frac{9}{4}{/tex} R2 - 6R2 cos {tex}\theta{/tex}
As d has to be greater than 2R, d2 > 4R2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4R2 + {tex}\frac{9}{4}{/tex} R2 - 6R2 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} > 4R2
or, {tex}\frac{9}{4}{/tex} > 6 cos {tex}\theta{/tex}  thus the conditions on the direction of momentum will the orbits be non-intersecting circles  is cos {tex}\theta{/tex}  < {tex}\frac{3}{8}{/tex}

question_text: A uniform conducting wire of length 12a and resistance R is wound up as a current-carrying coil in the shape of an equilateral triangle of side a, a square of sides a and, a regular hexagon of sides a. The coil is connected to a voltage source V 0 . Find the magnetic moment of the coils in each case.
question_html:
A uniform conducting wire of length 12a and resistance R is wound up as a current-carrying coil in the shape of

  1. an equilateral triangle of side a,
  2. a square of sides a and,
  3. a regular hexagon of sides a.

The coil is connected to a voltage source V0. Find the magnetic moment of the coils in each case.
solution_text: Key concept: In this problem different shapes form figures of different area and the number of loops in each case is different and hence, there magnetic moments varies. Magnetic moment is m = nlA. since, the same wire is used in three cases with same potentials, therefore, same current flows in three cases. A uniform conducting wire of length 12a and resistance R is wound up as a current carrying coil thus For an equilateral triangle of side a, As the total wire of length = 12 a, so, the no. of loops n {tex}=\frac{12 a}{3 a}{/tex} = 4. As area of triangle is A {tex}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} a^{2}{/tex} {tex}=4 I\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} a^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore m=I a^{2} \sqrt{3}{/tex} For a square of sides a, A = a 2 No. of loops n {tex}=\frac{12 a}{4 a}{/tex} = 3 Magnetic moment of the coils m = nIA = 3I(a2) = 3Ia 2 For a regular hexagon of sides a, No.of loops n {tex}=\frac{12 a}{6 a}{/tex} = 2 Area, {tex}A=\frac{6 \sqrt{3}}{4} a^{2}{/tex} Magnetic moment of the coils m = nIA {tex}\Rightarrow \quad m=2 I\left(\frac{6 \sqrt{3}}{4} a^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m = 3 {tex}\sqrt {3}{/tex} a 2 I, m is in a geometric series
solution_html:
Key concept: In this problem different shapes form figures of different area and the number of loops in each case is different
and hence, there magnetic moments varies.
Magnetic moment is m = nlA.
since, the same wire is used in three cases with same potentials, therefore, same current flows in three cases. A uniform conducting wire of length 12a and resistance R is wound up as a current carrying coil thus

  1. For an equilateral triangle of side a,
    As the total wire of length = 12 a, so, the no. of loops n {tex}=\frac{12 a}{3 a}{/tex} = 4.
    As area of triangle is A {tex}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} a^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}=4 I\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} a^{2}\right){/tex}
    {tex}\therefore m=I a^{2} \sqrt{3}{/tex}
  2. For a square of sides a,
    A = a2
    No. of loops n {tex}=\frac{12 a}{4 a}{/tex} = 3
    Magnetic moment of the coils m = nIA = 3I(a2) = 3Ia2
  3. For a regular hexagon of sides a, 
    No.of  loops n {tex}=\frac{12 a}{6 a}{/tex} = 2
    Area, {tex}A=\frac{6 \sqrt{3}}{4} a^{2}{/tex}
    Magnetic moment of the coils m = nIA
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad m=2 I\left(\frac{6 \sqrt{3}}{4} a^{2}\right){/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m = 3 {tex}\sqrt {3}{/tex} a2 I, m is in a geometric series

question_text: Consider a circular current-carrying loop of radius R in the x-y plane with centre at origin. Consider the line intergral {tex}\Im {/tex} (L) = {tex}\left|\int_{-L}^{L} \mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{d} \mathbf{l}\right|{/tex} taken along z-axis. Show that {tex}\Im {/tex} (L) monotonically increases with L. Use an appropriate Amperian loop to show that {tex}\Im {/tex} ( {tex}\infty{/tex} ) {tex}=\mu_{0} I{/tex} , where I is the current in the wire. Verify directly the above result. Suppose we replace the circular coil by a square coil of sides R carrying the same current I. What can you say about {tex}\Im {/tex} (L) and {tex}\Im {/tex} ( {tex}\infty{/tex} ) ?
question_html:
Consider a circular current-carrying loop of radius R in the x-y plane with centre at origin. Consider the line intergral {tex}\Im {/tex} (L) = {tex}\left|\int_{-L}^{L} \mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{d} \mathbf{l}\right|{/tex} taken along z-axis.

  1. Show that {tex}\Im {/tex} (L) monotonically increases with L.
  2. Use an appropriate Amperian loop to show that {tex}\Im {/tex} ({tex}\infty{/tex}) {tex}=\mu_{0} I{/tex}, where I is the current in the wire.
  3. Verify directly the above result.
  4. Suppose we replace the circular coil by a square coil of sides R carrying the same current I. What can you say about {tex}\Im {/tex}(L) and {tex}\Im {/tex}({tex}\infty{/tex}) ?
solution_text: Magnetic field due to a circular current-carrying loop lying in the xy- plane acts along z-axis as shown in figure. {tex}\Im(L)=\left|\int_{-L}^{L} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}\right|=\int_{-L}^{+L}{/tex} B {tex}dl{/tex} cos 0° {tex}=\int_{-L}^{+L} {/tex} Bdl= 2BL {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Im{/tex} (L) is monotonically increasing function of L. Now consider an Amperean loop around the circular coil of such a large radius L {tex}\rightarrow \infty{/tex} . since this loop encloses a current l, Now using Ampere's law {tex}\Im(\infty)=\oint^{+\infty} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0} I{/tex} The magnetic field at the axis (z-axis) of circular coil at a distance z from the centre of a circular coil of radius R carrying current I is given by {tex}B_{z}=\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(z^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} Now integrating {tex}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} B_{z} d z=\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(z^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} d z{/tex} Let z = R tan {tex}\theta{/tex} so that dz = R sec 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} d {tex}\theta{/tex} and (z 2 + R 2 ) 3/2 = (R 2 tan 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} + R 2 ) 3/2 = R 3 sec 3 {tex}\theta{/tex} (as 1 + tan 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} = sec 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} ) Thus, {tex}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} B_{z} d z=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2} \int_{-\pi / 2}^{+\pi / 2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{R^{2}\left(R \sec ^{2} \theta d \theta\right)}{R^{3} \sec ^{3} \theta}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2} \int_{-\pi / 2}^{+\pi / 2} \cos \theta d \theta=\mu_{0} I{/tex} As we know (B Z ) square < (B z ) circular coil For the same current, and side of the square equal to radius of the coil {tex}\Im(\infty)_{\text {square }}<\Im(\infty)_{\text {circularcoil }}{/tex} By using the same argument as we done in case (b), it can be shown that {tex}\Im(\infty)_{\text {square }}=\Im(\infty)_{\text {circularcoil }}=\mu_0 I{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Magnetic field due to a circular current-carrying loop lying in the xy- plane acts along z-axis as shown in figure.
    {tex}\Im(L)=\left|\int_{-L}^{L} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}\right|=\int_{-L}^{+L}{/tex} B{tex}dl{/tex}cos 0° {tex}=\int_{-L}^{+L} {/tex} Bdl= 2BL
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Im{/tex} (L) is monotonically increasing function of L.
  2. Now consider an Amperean loop around the circular coil of such a large radius L {tex}\rightarrow \infty{/tex}. since this loop encloses a current l, Now using Ampere's law
    {tex}\Im(\infty)=\oint^{+\infty} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0} I{/tex}
  3. The magnetic field at the axis (z-axis) of circular coil at a distance z from the centre of a circular coil of radius R carrying current I is
    given  by {tex}B_{z}=\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(z^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex}
    Now integrating
    {tex}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} B_{z} d z=\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(z^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} d z{/tex}
    Let z = R tan {tex}\theta{/tex} so that dz = R sec2 {tex}\theta{/tex} d{tex}\theta{/tex}
    and (z2 + R2)3/2 = (R2 tan2 {tex}\theta{/tex} + R2)3/2
    = R3 sec3 {tex}\theta{/tex} (as 1 + tan2{tex}\theta{/tex} = sec2{tex}\theta{/tex})
    Thus, {tex}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} B_{z} d z=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2} \int_{-\pi / 2}^{+\pi / 2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{R^{2}\left(R \sec ^{2} \theta d \theta\right)}{R^{3} \sec ^{3} \theta}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2} \int_{-\pi / 2}^{+\pi / 2} \cos \theta d \theta=\mu_{0} I{/tex}
  4. As we know (BZ) square < (Bz) circular coil
    For the same current, and side of the square equal to radius of the coil
    {tex}\Im(\infty)_{\text {square }}<\Im(\infty)_{\text {circularcoil }}{/tex}
    By using the same argument as we done in case (b), it can be shown that
    {tex}\Im(\infty)_{\text {square }}=\Im(\infty)_{\text {circularcoil }}=\mu_0 I{/tex}

question_text: Five long wires A, B, C, D and E, each carrying current I are arranged to form edges of a pentagonal prism as shown in Fig. Each carries current out of the plane of paper. What will be magnetic induction at a point on the axis O? Axis is at a distance R from each wire. What will be the field if current in one of the wires (say A) is switched off? What if current in one of the wire (say) A is reversed?
question_html:
Five long wires A, B, C, D and E, each carrying current I are arranged to form edges of a pentagonal prism as shown in Fig. Each carries current out of the plane of paper.

  1. What will be magnetic induction at a point on the axis O? Axis is at a distance R from each wire.
  2. What will be the field if current in one of the wires (say A) is switched off?
  3. What if current in one of the wire (say) A is reversed?
solution_text: Suppose the five wires A, B, C, D and E be perpendicular to the plane of paper at locations as shown in figure. Thus, magnetic field induction due to five wires will be represented by various sides of closed pentagon in one order lying in the plane of paper. So, its value is zero. Total Magnetic field induction at O due to currents through the wires A, B, C, D and E is equal and opposite to the magnetic field induction at O due to current through wire A. since, the vector sum of magnetic field produced by each wire at O is equal to 0. Therefore, magnetic induction produced by one current carrying wire is equal magnitude of resultant of four wires and opposite in direction. {tex}\vec{B}_{A}+\vec{B}_{\text {remaining }}=0{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi R} \hat{i}+\hat{B}_{\text {remaining }} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \hat{B}_{\text {remaining }}=-\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi R} \hat{i}{/tex} Therefore, the field if current in one of the wires (say A) is switched off is {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \frac{i}{R}{/tex} perpendicular to AO towards left. If current in wire A is reversed, then Total magnetie field induction at O = Magnetic field induction due to wire A + Magnetic field induction due to wires B, C, D, and E {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi } \frac{2 I}{R}{/tex} (acting perpendicular to AO towards left) {tex}+\frac{\mu_{0}}{4\pi} \frac{2 I}{R}{/tex} (acting perpendicular AO towards left)= {tex}\mu_0I\over \pi R{/tex} acting perpendicular AO towards left
solution_html:
1. Suppose the five wires A, B, C, D and E be perpendicular to the plane of paper at locations as shown in figure.
    Thus, magnetic field induction due to five wires will be represented by various sides of closed pentagon in one order lying in the plane of paper. So, its value is zero.
  2. Total Magnetic field induction at O due to currents through the wires A, B, C, D and E is equal and opposite to the magnetic field induction at O due to current through wire A. since, the vector sum of magnetic field produced by each wire at O is equal to 0. Therefore, magnetic induction produced by one current carrying wire is equal magnitude of resultant of four wires and opposite in direction.
    {tex}\vec{B}_{A}+\vec{B}_{\text {remaining }}=0{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi R} \hat{i}+\hat{B}_{\text {remaining }} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \hat{B}_{\text {remaining }}=-\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi R} \hat{i}{/tex}
    Therefore, the field if current in one of the wires (say A) is switched off is {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \frac{i}{R}{/tex} perpendicular to AO towards left.
  3. If current in wire A is reversed, then
    Total magnetie field induction at O
    = Magnetic field induction due to wire A + Magnetic field induction due to wires B, C, D, and E
    {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi } \frac{2 I}{R}{/tex} (acting perpendicular to AO towards left) {tex}+\frac{\mu_{0}}{4\pi} \frac{2 I}{R}{/tex}(acting perpendicular AO towards left)={tex}\mu_0I\over \pi R{/tex}acting perpendicular AO towards left
